column2: The Alternative Fuels Demonstration Program (formerly the Synthetic Fuels Commercialization Program) is a federal effort to prove that fuels made from coal, oil shale, and organic wastes can be produced at commercial scale. The program would build large plants using technologies such as high- and low-Btu coal gasification, coal liquefaction, oil shale surface and in-place processing, and solid waste pyrolysis. Production options range from 350,000 to 1.7 million barrels per day of oil-equivalent fuel by 1985, supported by federal incentives like loan guarantees. Plants would be built across f…
column7: The extraction is broadly accurate and well-supported by the cited pages. Two minor concerns: (1) the specific claim of '49 written comments' and 'January 1976' draft filing date are not verifiable f…

column2: The program would affect six regions: the Appalachian, Eastern Interior, Fort Union, Powder River, and Four Corners coal regions, and the Oil Shale Region in Colorado, Utah, and Wyoming. Permanent population increases could reach tens of thousands per region, with the largest impacts in sparsely populated rural Western counties and on Indian reservations including the Navajo, Crow, Northern Cheyenne, Fort Peck, Fort Berthold, and Uintah and Ouray. Many affected counties have predominantly blue-collar workforces, below-average incomes, aging or substandard housing, and economies dependent on a…

Thirteen main alternatives are evaluated, including no action (increased oil and gas imports or accepting an energy shortfall), direct use of coal, increased energy conservation, nuclear, geothermal, solar, wind, hydroelectric, tidal, tar sands, more domestic oil and gas, a hydrogen economy, and energy from solid wastes. Different program sizes are also analyzed, from 350,000 up to 1.7 million barrels per day, along with $2, $4, and $6 billion budget options. Except for varying production levels, none of the alternatives would meet all program goals, and renewables like solar, wind, geotherma…

Plants would emit sulfur oxides, nitrogen oxides, particulates, hydrocarbons, carbon monoxide, aldehydes, and trace amounts of toxic metals and polycyclic aromatic compounds that may pose chronic health risks, including cancer. Water demand is large—up to about 60,000 acre-feet per year per plant for some technologies—and could strain limited supplies in the Powder River, Fort Union, Four Corners, and Oil Shale regions, while raising salinity in the Colorado River and threatening groundwater from mining and spent shale leachate. Land impacts include up to roughly 171,000 acres disturbed cumul…

After the Draft EIS was filed in January 1976, ERDA received 49 written comments during and after the two-month public comment period from federal agencies, many state governments, industry groups, environmental organizations, and Indian tribes, in addition to testimony at public meetings and Congressional hearings. In response, the Final EIS was substantially revised, expanding analysis of water availability and quality, socioeconomic effects, community growth mitigation, surface mine reclamation, and coal slurry pipelines.
